topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara let out a sharp, involuntary gasp, clutching the blueprints to her chest. The fluorescent lights of the shared lab seemed to hum with an extra voltage, mirroring the sudden jolt that shot through her. She spun around, her eyes wide, scanning the cluttered workbench where a nearly finished schematic lay beside a half-empty mug of lukewarm coffee. Beside it, a notebook, open to the exact same page, detailed the precise specifications of the device she had just been sketching. A slow, breathless smile spread across her face, the kind that makes your cheeks feel warm. "No way," she whispered, her voice tight with disbelief as she looked toward the doorway, expecting to see someone else.

[story 2]

Mark tapped his pen against the worn leather cover of his journal, a quick, rhythmic sound against the silence of the workshop. He leaned back in his chair, a stretch running through his shoulders, and his gaze drifted across the room. His breath hitched. There, on the central table, lay the prototype casing—the very design he had labored over for weeks. Next to it, a handwritten logbook, its script flowing with an identical cadence to his own entries. A surge of pure, unadulterated excitement made his hands tremble slightly as he reached out, his fingers brushing against the identical ink. "This is impossible," he murmured, a wide grin pulling at the corners of his mouth as he stared from the physical objects to the empty space where he was certain someone else should be.

[story 3]

A bright spark ignited behind Chloe’s eyes as she carefully laid the finished component onto the testing platform. She stepped back, her posture straightening, a buoyant energy radiating from her. Then, her attention snagged on the adjacent station. There, under the same harsh overhead lamp, sat a nearly identical assembly, the wiring diagrams mirroring hers with unnerving precision. Chloe let out a soft, delighted laugh, a sound bubbling up from deep in her chest. She walked over, her steps light and quick, her fingers tracing the edges of the identical casing. "Well, hello there," she said, her tone pitched high with sheer fascination as she surveyed the uncanny duplication.
